data.title: Euro Area inflation drops to 1.9% in December
data.description:
• The inflation rate in the Euro Area decreased to 1.90% in December from 2.1% last month, lower than estimates of 2%.

  • The CPI in the Euro Area increased 0.2% M/M in December 2025, rebounding from a 0.3% fall in November, matching the initial estimate.

data.title: South Korea to discuss favorable chip tariff terms with US
data.description:
South Korea will look for favorable terms for U.S. tariffs on imports of memory chips, said a presidential office spokesperson on Sunday at a televised briefing, Reuters reported.

Last year, the Asian country released a joint fact sheet on its trade deal with the U.S., which included terms ensuring South Korea will not receive unfavorable treatment from American tariffs on imported chips compared to key competitors, said the official when asked about the Trump administration's announcement imposing tariffs on AI chips, the report added.

On Saturday, South Korea's trade minister said U.S. tariffs on some advanced computing chips would have a limited impact on the companies from the Asian nation, the report noted.

Over the weekend it was reported that Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that memory chipmakers, including those from South Korea and Taiwan, could face 100% tariffs on their imports unless they boost their manufacturing capabilities in the U.S.

 South Korean companies Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F) are among the largest memory chipmaking companies in the world. Among the several memory products the companies make are high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, chips, which are vital to make advanced accelerators for AI. SK hynix is a major supplier of HBM chips to Nvidia (NVDA).

data.title: Europe’s biggest carmakers shares fall on Trump’s tariff threat over Greenland
data.description:
Shares of some of Europe’s biggest carmakers fell on Monday morning, following U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest tariff threats on several European countries over Greenland.

Europe’s Stoxx Automobiles and Parts index traded over 2% lower at around 9:30 a.m. Central European Time.

Germany’s Volkswagen, BMW, and Mercedes-Benz Group were trading between 2.5% to 4% lower shortly after the opening bell. Milan-listed shares of Stellantis and Ferrari were also trading lower.

This comes after Trump on Saturday threatened up to 25% of tariffs against eight European countries, including the U.K., France, Germany, and Denmark, which have deployed military in Greenland at a time when the U.S. is aiming to take ownership of the Arctic Island.

According to Trump, the countries, some of which are among the closest allies of the U.S., face a 10% tariff from Feb. 1, and from June 1, the levies will rise to a 25% rate unless an agreement is reached before then.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) also dropped on Monday after Trump's tariff threats.

The European Union is reportedly also weighing imposing €93B ($108.2B) worth of duties on U.S. imports in response to the tariff threats.

data.title: Tesla set to be early winner of Canada-China EV tariff deal - report
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) will likely be one of the first automakers to benefit from Canada's move to slash tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, Reuters reported.

Canada will allow 49,000 EVs to be imported from China annually with a 6.1% tariff, down from the 100% rate that was in place since 2024, in exchange for tariff concessions on canola and other Canadian goods. The EV quota could rise to 70,000 within five years.

While Chinese EV makers don't have a significant sales presence in Canada, Tesla (TSLA) has an existing network of 39 stores in the country.

Tesla's (TSLA) biggest plant is in Shanghai, which is already equipped to build and export a Canada-specific version of its Model Y, according to the report.

It started shipping this version to Canada in 2023 but was forced to stop in 2024 after Ottawa imposed 100% tariffs on Chinese-made EVs. It currently ships Model Ys produced in Berlin to Canada.

To note, half of Canada's EV quota under the trade deal will be reserved for vehicles priced under C$35,000 ($25,192). Tesla (TSLA) model prices are above that number.

Low-cost cars from China's BYD (BYDDF) could benefit from that quota, but the company currently has negligible sales in Canada. 

Other automakers that exported Chinese-made cars to Canada before the imposition of 100% tariffs include Volvo (VLVLY) and Polestar (PSNY), which are both owned by Chinese conglomerate Geely (GELYF).

data.title: Prediction markets odds above 50% that Kevin Warsh likely to be next Fed chair
data.description:
Kevin Warsh is the clear favorite to become the next chair of the U.S. Federal Reserve, according to betting markets, with implied probabilities clustered in the mid-to-high 50% range.

On Kalshi, Warsh is priced at 55%, while Polymarket places his odds at 56% and Gemini at 57%, giving him an aggregated probability of about 56%, data show.

Rick Rieder ranks a distant second, with odds near 20% across platforms, followed by Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller at roughly 13–14%. Rieder has seen a sharp pickup in momentum, rising 5.6% in the last 24 hours.

Kevin Hassett trails the field with probabilities around 6–7%. President Donald Trump on Friday said he wants to keep former favorite Kevin Hassett as director of the White House National Economic Council.

data.title: EU said to weigh €93B tariffs on U.S. in response to Trump's Greenland threat
data.description:
The European Union is weighing imposing €93B ($108.2B) worth of duties on U.S. imports in response to President Donald Trump's latest tariff threat to countries that sent troops to Greenland, The Financial Times reported.

The tariffs had been prepared last year, but were suspended till Feb. 6 under the EU's trade deal with the U.S. The EU's 27 ambassadors discussed reactivating the tariffs on Sunday, officials involved in the talks told FT.

They also discussed the bloc's "anti-coercion instrument," also called the trade bazooka, which can limit the access of U.S. companies to the internal market.

France, which has backed using the ACI, and Germany are coordinating a joint response. Their finance ministers will discuss the matter in Berlin on Monday and then travel to Brussels for a meeting with their European counterparts.

But most EU member states are calling for dialogue with Trump before threatening retaliation. "We need to get the temperature down," an EU diplomat told the FT.

The proposed retaliation measures are aimed at giving EU leaders leverage when they meet Trump at the World Economic Forum in Davos this week.

"In 2025, Europe generally avoided retaliatory measures, recognizing the harm tariffs can do to the domestic economy," said UBS' Paul Donovan. "Targeted measures, including the restricting of U.S. social media providers and other services, are now more likely."

data.title: European markets slide after Trump's tariff threats
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.26% to 10,208. 

Germany (DAX:IND) -1.26% to 24,985. 

France (CAC:IND) -1.47% to 8,137. 

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) dropped 1.20% to around €607, as renewed tariff threats from U.S. President Donald Trump weighed on global risk sentiment. His comments linked to Greenland got trade policy talks back to attention, pressuring more cyclical parts of the market. Trump has threatened 10% tariffs on several EU countries to pressure Denmark into selling Greenland.

The euro moved higher to $1.162, recovering from a three-month low of $1.158, as investor confidence in the dollar weakened. 

Coming up in the session: Euro Area inflation lined up shortly.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 7 basis points to 4.23%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was almost flat at 2.83%.

The UK's 10-year yield was up less than 1 basis point to 4.41%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Asia stocks slide as Trump’s Greenland tariffs ignite global trade fears; China meets 5% annual growth goal
Is the stock market open on Martin Luther King Jr. Day?
data.description:
U.S. stock and bond markets are closed on Monday, Jan. 19, in observance of Martin Luther King Jr. Day.

The New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq are both shut, though stock futures continue to trade. Futures tied to the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) were last down -0.63%, S&P 500 (SPX) futures fell -0.7%, and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) declined -0.92%. 

All three major indexes ended with marginal losses on Friday, as investors digested a mix of geopolitical developments, Federal Reserve uncertainty, and the start of the fourth-quarter earnings season.

Metals and energy futures remain active on Comex. Spot gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) climbed about 1.7% to $4,672.61 an ounce, hitting a record high as investors sought safe-haven assets after U.S. President Donald Trump announced new tariffs on several European nations tied to the Greenland territorial dispute. Silver spot price (XAGUSD:CUR) also gained +3.9% to $93.47 an ounce.

Asian equity markets mostly traded lower amid renewed trade uncertainty. Japan (NKY:IND) slid 1.3%; Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.02%; India (SENSEX) fell 0.68%; and Australia (AS51) fell 0.33% to 8,886.

Cryptocurrency markets remain open, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) last trading at $92,550.

Martin Luther King Jr. Day honors civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr., who was born on Jan. 15. The federal holiday, observed on the third Monday of January, was signed into law by President Ronald Reagan in 1983 and took effect three years later. All U.S. states officially recognized the holiday by 1991, while New Hampshire became the last state to adopt King’s name for the observance in 1999, after previously calling it “Civil Rights Day.”

data.title: State Street anticipates 4–6% fee revenue growth and 100+ basis points positive operating leverage in 2026 as AI and digital transformation accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: State Street Corporation (STT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ronald O’Hanley, CEO & Chairman, stated, "Our fourth quarter results represent a strong finish to another successful year for State Street. We entered 2026 with momentum and a proven strategy that continues to deliver strong results and meaningful value for our clients, shareholders and employees." He highlighted record quarterly fee and total revenue, 14% year-over-year EPS growth, and pretax margin expansion to 31% for the quarter, excluding notable items. The company achieved its eighth straight quarter of positive operating leverage and completed its $500 million productivity savings target for the full year, cumulatively reaching nearly $2 billion in savings over five years. State Street’s Investment Services surpassed $50 trillion in AUC/A, while the private market servicing business grew servicing fees at a double-digit rate. O’Hanley also announced the launch of a digital asset platform and emphasized strategic advances in wealth services via a partnership and minority investment in Apex Fintech Solutions.
  • O’Hanley described the Investment Management business as having achieved record management fee revenue and consistent net new asset growth, ending the year with $5.7 trillion in AUM. Product innovation was underscored by the launch of 134 new products, including alternatives and ETF offerings in partnership with Apollo, Bridgewater, and Blackstone, and several strategic investments in technology and distribution partners in the U.S., Europe, and India.
  • O’Hanley reported double-digit fee revenue growth in FX trading services and securities finance within State Street Markets. He concluded, "2025 marked several strategic and performance milestones for State Street, reinforcing the effectiveness of our strategy as we enter 2026 with clear momentum."
  • John Woods, EVP & CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter highlights included year-over-year fee revenue growth of 8%, higher net interest income and net interest margin, continued capital return and robust EPS growth of 14%. Our pretax margin in the quarter improved to approximately 31%, return on tangible common equity increased to 22%, and we generated operating leverage of over 100 basis points." Woods reported record total revenue of approximately $14 billion for the year, a 7% increase from the prior year, and expenses rising 5% to $9.8 billion, primarily due to strategic and technology investments.

Outlook

  • Woods stated, "We currently expect fee revenue to be up 4% to 6%, driven by continued momentum in servicing and management fees, reflecting higher average market levels and organic growth, and supported by continued solid client engagement in our markets business." He added, "We expect NII to be up low single digits for the full year, off a record 2025 print, with an expected improvement in net interest margin relative to 2025." Expenses are projected to rise approximately 3% to 4%, with productivity and other savings largely offsetting recurring operating cost growth. Woods projected "positive operating leverage in excess of 100 basis points in 2026," with pretax margin improvement to roughly 30%.

Financial Results

  • Fee revenue grew 8% year-over-year in Q4. Servicing fees increased 8%, with record AUC/A of $53.8 trillion, up 16% year-over-year. The private markets business grew 12% in 2025, representing about 10% of servicing fees. Management fees reached a quarterly record of $662 million, with AUM at $5.7 trillion, up 20% year-over-year. The company launched 37 new investment management products in the fourth quarter alone. State Street Markets saw FX trading revenue rise 13% and securities finance revenues increase 8% year-over-year. Software and processing fees declined 15% due to lower on-premises renewals, while SaaS revenue grew 7% and annual recurring revenue increased 11% to about $420 million. Net interest income for the quarter was $802 million, up 7% year-over-year, with net interest margin expanding to 1.10%. The company returned $635 million of capital to shareholders in Q4, including $400 million in repurchases and $235 million in dividends.

Q&A

  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore: Asked about balancing investment and operating leverage, and the impact of AI. Woods explained that productivity savings are enabling investments in strategic initiatives, and AI will increasingly contribute to efficiency, especially in the second half of 2026 and beyond. O’Hanley emphasized, "We are investing at a very high level...this is the moment we believe we should be investing in capabilities of technology, et cetera."
  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley: Probed digital assets strategy and timing of financial impact. O’Hanley responded the focus is on digitalization and tokenization of assets, not crypto, and the financial impact will be a medium-term story rather than immediately visible in 2026. Woods added, "It's not really going to be visible in '26, it's more of a medium-term matter."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein: Inquired about NII sustainability and balance sheet optimization. Woods noted seasonal factors in deposit mix benefited Q4 and expects some moderation, with net interest margin projected to rise in 2026.
  • James Mitchell, Seaport: Asked about balance sheet optimization. Woods described running off higher-cost wholesale funding and focusing lending on deep customer relationships, with deposits expected to remain around $250 billion, including about 10% noninterest-bearing.
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Queried organic growth in fee businesses. Woods confirmed organic growth is embedded in 2026 expectations and noted the transition to SaaS in software may create near-term headwinds but will benefit long-term recurring revenue.
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Sought clarity on SaaS transition and loan growth. Woods said the SaaS transition will take a year or two to show revenue growth, and loan growth is expected to continue but at a slower pace than 2025.
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about outperformance against guidance and impact of Basel III Endgame. O’Hanley cited conservative initial guidance and better-than-expected execution, with regulatory changes expected to be favorable.
  • David Smith, Truist: Explored future ROTCE potential. O’Hanley and Woods indicated confidence in further improvements, emphasizing innovation and efficiency gains.
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Challenged management on stock performance and long-term strategic conviction. O’Hanley outlined five reasons to own State Street, highlighting industry fundamentals, distinctive capabilities, execution record, management strength, and capital-light business model. Woods added, "All that upside we think should accrue to the benefit of the existing shareholder base."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts adopted a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, challenging management on the sustainability of recent performance, digital asset strategy, organic growth, and long-term shareholder value. Questions pressed on near-term financial impact, stock valuation, and strategic alternatives, with Mayo highlighting investor frustration.
  • Management was confident and optimistic in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing momentum, innovation, and the benefits of technology and productivity savings. O’Hanley and Woods consistently reiterated the company’s long-term strategic vision and adaptability, with phrases such as "we are confident" and "we believe."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism was more pronounced, particularly concerning valuation and future growth translation to shareholder returns, while management maintained a similar or slightly more assertive tone in defending strategy and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from specific fee revenue and NII growth targets in Q3 to broader, scenario-based ranges for 2026 in Q4. Q4 guidance incorporated more explicit expectations for AI-led transformation and digital assets positioning. Strategic focus expanded with new digital asset and wealth services initiatives. Management tone remained confident, but with heightened emphasis on execution and differentiation. Analysts’ questions in Q4 more directly addressed long-term strategy and stock underperformance, while in Q3 the focus was more on operational improvements and backlog installations. Key metrics such as AUC/A, AUM, and fee revenue continued their growth trajectory, while expense growth guidance ticked higher to support investment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified uncertainty in timing and scale of financial impact from digital asset initiatives. The transition from on-premises to SaaS in software and processing may pressure near-term revenues. Expense growth, if not offset by productivity gains, could threaten margins. Regulatory developments (Basel III Endgame) and market volatility remain external risks. Analysts expressed concerns over the sustainability of organic growth, competitive pressures in software, and whether recent momentum will translate into sustained shareholder value.

Final Takeaway

State Street’s 2025 performance was characterized by record revenues, robust fee growth, and expanding margins. The company enters 2026 with a focus on further digital transformation, AI-driven productivity, and strategic expansion in wealth and digital assets. Management projects continued positive operating leverage and fee growth, underpinned by disciplined investment and technology innovation, while acknowledging that some strategic initiatives will deliver medium-term rather than immediate financial impact. The leadership team emphasized confidence in their strategy, adaptability to market conditions, and commitment to delivering long-term value to clients and shareholders.
